takeaways: Senior housing operators are riding record fundamentals — Welltower posted Q1 2026 SHOP NOI up 16.4% and raised full-year guidance, while Ventas grew occupancy 370 bps in the U.S. and deployed $1.7B YTD — even as new construction sinks to its lowest level since 2012.
Capital is reopening: the Fed holds rates steady after three cuts with a midyear move expected, banks are re-entering CRE lending, and Colliers projects 15–20% higher sales volume in 2026.

AI is reshaping proptech and the C-suite alike — VC poured $16.7B into proptech in 2025 (+68% YoY), three new AI unicorns have minted since July, and CEO ownership of AI strategy has doubled in a year.

items.headline: Senior living construction starts hit lowest level since 2012
items.summary: New units under construction fell to levels not seen since 2012, with the industry adding just 0.4% of total inventory in Q1 2026. Tariffs, construction labor shortages, and persistent cost pressures are tempering 2026 development — a constrained supply pipeline that supports occupancy and pricing power for existing Lloyd Jones communities.

items.headline: Welltower posts record senior housing NOI, raises 2026 guidance
items.summary: Welltower reported Q1 2026 FFO of $1.47/share and revenue up 38.3% year over year, with same-store NOI up 16.4% led by its senior housing operating portfolio and occupancy reaching 88.8% (nearly 400 bps of U.S. gains). Management raised full-year normalized FFO guidance, signaling the strongest senior housing operating environment in years.

items.headline: Ventas grows senior housing occupancy 370 bps, deploys $1.7B
items.summary: Ventas reported Q1 2026 SHOP same-store cash NOI up more than 15% YoY, with U.S. average occupancy up 370 bps and RevPOR up 5%. The REIT closed $1.0B of senior housing investments in Q1 and $1.7B YTD through April — over 90% relationship-driven — underscoring fierce competition for quality senior housing assets.

items.headline: REITs dominate off-market senior living M&A as new capital enters
items.summary: Competition for senior living assets remains intense in 2026, with REITs winning off-market deals on speed. New entrants are pushing in too, including Chiron Real Estate's $425M investment in three luxury senior properties. For operators like Lloyd Jones, this signals strong asset valuations and a deep buyer pool for well-run communities.

items.headline: Federal bill would make assisted living a Medicaid benefit
items.summary: The proposed Assisted Living ACCESS Act would make assisted living a covered Medicaid benefit for older adults meeting nursing-facility-level care criteria. Backers say it expands access while lowering federal spending, but the industry warns of unintended reimbursement and compliance consequences — a regulatory shift senior housing operators should track closely.

items.headline: Senior living at 'inflection point' with $300M–$600M pipeline
items.summary: A senior living transformation company says it is ready to expand with a $300M–$600M acquisition and development pipeline, calling 2026 an 'inflection point' as demand from aging demographics outpaces thin supply. The momentum reflects reopening capital and rising investor conviction in the sector's long-term tailwinds.

items.headline: Fed holds rates steady; midyear cut expected to ease CRE
items.summary: The Fed entered 2026 holding rates steady after three 25-bps cuts, with markets broadly expecting another adjustment by midyear. But the 10-Year Treasury — which prices most fixed-rate CRE loans — has stabilized around 4.00%–4.25%, meaning borrowing costs may ease only gradually for senior housing acquisitions and refinancings.

items.headline: Colliers projects 15–20% jump in 2026 CRE sales volume
items.summary: Capital is flowing back into commercial real estate as institutional and cross-border investors re-enter the market. Colliers forecasts a 15–20% increase in 2026 sales volume, with recent quarterly volume up more than 40% YoY and banks easing back into lending — a more constructive deal environment for senior housing transactions.

items.headline: Banks re-enter CRE debt markets as liquidity returns
items.summary: Banks are expected to reemerge as active CRE lenders in 2026, joining alternative lenders as the yield curve steepens. Liquidity is described as abundant and the lending environment healthy — though default rates are still rising for obsolete assets. Better lender participation should improve financing access for quality senior housing deals.

items.headline: Commercial loan rates span 5.23%–12.75% in mid-May 2026
items.summary: As of May 19, 2026, commercial loan rates ranged from roughly 5.23% to 12.75%, with conventional commercial mortgage rates between 5.48% and 8.92% depending on property type and borrower credit. The wide band underscores how asset quality and sponsor strength drive financing terms in today's market.

items.headline: Proptech VC hits $16.7B in 2025, with AI growth doubling rivals
items.summary: Venture firms invested $16.7B in proptech in 2025, up 67.9% from 2024. Investment in AI-centered proptech grew at a 42% annualized rate — nearly double the 24% growth for non-AI companies. The capital surge is accelerating AI tools for underwriting, rent rolls, inspections and reconciliation that senior housing operators can deploy.

items.headline: Proptech funding surges 176% YoY as three AI unicorns emerge
items.summary: Firms poured roughly $1.7B into proptech in January 2026, a 176% increase over January 2025. Three new proptech unicorns have been minted since July, and all offer AI solutions automating real estate processes — evidence that investor appetite for operational AI in property management is intensifying.

items.headline: Agentic AI emerges as the next phase of proptech
items.summary: Agentic AI is being positioned as proptech's next frontier, automating complex workflows across procurement, pricing, inventory and resident programming. As AI moves from experimentation to delivery in 2026, senior housing operators have a near-term opportunity to cut operating costs and improve resident engagement at scale.

items.headline: Proptech turns AI against rising rental and mortgage fraud
items.summary: Owners and proptech firms are deploying AI to fight a surge in AI-driven rental and mortgage fraud. The FBI logged more than 12,000 real estate fraud complaints in 2025, totaling over $275M in reported losses. Identity-verification and document-screening tools are becoming essential safeguards for leasing operations.

items.headline: US layoffs hit 10-year high as 71% of leaders report more stress
items.summary: Layoffs in the U.S. have surged to their highest level in a decade, and 71% of leaders report increased stress — with 40% of stressed leaders considering quitting. Economic pressure and AI-driven efficiencies are flattening hierarchies, a dynamic that puts a premium on retention and leadership development at operating companies.

items.headline: CEO ownership of AI strategy doubles in a year
items.summary: Nearly three-quarters of CEOs now say they are their organization's main decision-maker on AI — double the share a year ago — and half believe their job is on the line if AI investments don't pay off. Companies plan to double AI spending in 2026, making AI fluency a core leadership competency.

items.headline: Leadership development is HR's top priority for 2026
items.summary: Leadership development remains HR's number-one priority heading into 2026, with enabling innovation rising fast alongside it. Most organizations are expected to operate with fewer management layers by 2030, raising the importance of building proactive leadership pipelines rather than reacting to gaps.

items.headline: 89% of employees say wellbeing lifts their performance
items.summary: Wellhub's State of Work-Life Wellness 2026 finds 89% of employees perform better when prioritizing wellbeing, reinforcing wellness as a driver of productivity and retention. Gyms, wellness studios and outdoor spaces are emerging as the new 'third places' — a cue for senior housing operators investing in resident and staff wellness amenities.

items.headline: Employee wellbeing slips: only 54% rate themselves thriving
items.summary: Despite rising focus on wellness, just 54% of workers rated their wellbeing as good or thriving in 2025, down from 63% in 2024. The decline highlights persistent burnout and points to mental health support, flexible policies and preventive care as the wellness investments most likely to move the needle.

items.headline: Longevity and metabolic health go mainstream in 2026
items.summary: Wellness in 2026 is shifting toward hyper-personalization — biomarker-based nutrition and AI wellness coaches — while longevity and metabolic health move from niche to everyday lifestyle. The five pillars of fitness, mindfulness, therapy, nutrition and sleep are reshaping consumer expectations, including among aging adults.
